examples.explanation: Start with max_sum = 0. Recursively explore: Index 0 (1): include (sum=1, last=1) → index 1: include 101 (valid, sum=102, last=101) → index 2: include 2 (invalid, 2<101) → skip → index 3: include 3 (invalid) → skip → index 4: include 100 (invalid) → skip → end, max_sum=102; backtrack: index 1 skip → index 2 include 2 (valid from 1, sum=3, last=2) → index 3 include 3 (valid, sum=6, last=3) → index 4 include 100 (valid, sum=106, last=100) → end, max_sum=106; continue exploring all paths. Final max_sum=106 from subsequence [1, 2, 3, 100].

Start max_sum=0. Explore: Index 0 include 3 (sum=3, last=3) → index 1 include 4 (valid, sum=7, last=4) → index 2 include 5 (valid, sum=12, last=5) → index 3 include 10 (valid, sum=22, last=10) → end, max_sum=22. Other paths yield smaller sums (e.g., [3,4,5]=12, [4,5,10]=19). Final max_sum=22 from entire array.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible subsequences recursively, check if each is increasing, and track the maximum sum. Use it for small inputs to understand the problem but avoid for larger datasets.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution generating all subsequences, checking if increasing, and tracking maximum sum.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the maximum sum increasing subsequence problem using brute force recursion. The intuition is to explore every possible subsequence of the input array, which grows exponentially (2^n possibilities). For each subsequence, we check if it's strictly increasing (each element greater than the previous), and if so, compute its sum. We track the maximum sum found across all valid increasing subsequences. The algorithm uses recursion with backtracking: at each index, we have two choices—include the current element in the subsequence (if it maintains the increasing property) or skip it. We recursively explore both paths, passing along the last included element's value and the current sum. The base case is when we reach the end of the array, where we update the global maximum if needed. Time complexity is O(2^n) because we generate all subsets, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This approach is foundational for understanding the problem but impractical for large n > 20 due to exponential growth. It connects to more efficient solutions (like dynamic programming) by revealing the overlapping subproblems. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays correctly, forgetting to check for strictly increasing sequences, or inefficiently recomputing sums. Real code pattern uses a helper function with parameters for current index, last value, and current sum.

examples.trace: Start with max_sum = 0. Recursively explore: Index 0 (1): include (sum=1, last=1) → index 1: include 101 (valid, sum=102, last=101) → index 2: include 2 (invalid, 2<101) → skip → index 3: include 3 (invalid) → skip → index 4: include 100 (invalid) → skip → end, max_sum=102; backtrack: index 1 skip → index 2 include 2 (valid from 1, sum=3, last=2) → index 3 include 3 (valid, sum=6, last=3) → index 4 include 100 (valid, sum=106, last=100) → end, max_sum=106; continue exploring all paths. Final max_sum=106 from subsequence [1, 2, 3, 100].

Start max_sum=0. Explore: Index 0 include 3 (sum=3, last=3) → index 1 include 4 (valid, sum=7, last=4) → index 2 include 5 (valid, sum=12, last=5) → index 3 include 10 (valid, sum=22, last=10) → end, max_sum=22. Other paths yield smaller sums (e.g., [3,4,5]=12, [4,5,10]=19). Final max_sum=22 from entire array.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Subsequence Generation with Increasing Constraint
ladders.desc: Helper function that recursively generates subsequences while ensuring they are increasing.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function for the brute force approach. The intuition is to traverse the array and at each step decide whether to include the current element in the subsequence, but only if it's greater than the last included element (to maintain increasing order). We use backtracking: include the element if valid, update the sum and last value, recurse to the next index; then backtrack by skipping the element and recursing. The function takes parameters: current index i, last included value last_val (or -inf if none), and current sum curr_sum. Base case: if i == n (array length), update a global max_sum with curr_sum if larger. Otherwise, recursively call for i+1 with skipping. Also, if arr[i] > last_val, recursively call for i+1 with including arr[i], updating last_val to arr[i] and curr_sum to curr_sum + arr[i]. Time complexity is O(2^n) as it explores all subsets, and space is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to explore all possibilities. Common mistakes: not initializing last_val properly for empty subsequence, using <= instead of < for strictly increasing, or not resetting state when backtracking. Real code pattern: def helper(i, last_val, curr_sum): if i==n: update max; else: helper(i+1, last_val, curr_sum); if arr[i] > last_val: helper(i+1, arr[i], curr_sum+arr[i]).

examples.trace: i=0: skip → call helper(1, -inf, 0); include 1 (valid) → call helper(1, 1, 1). From helper(1, -inf, 0): i=1: skip → base, sum=0; include 2 (valid) → base, sum=2. From helper(1, 1, 1): i=1: skip → base, sum=1; include 2 (valid, 2>1) → base, sum=3. Sums collected: 0,2,1,3.

i=0: skip → helper(1, -inf, 0); include 3 → helper(1, 3, 3). From helper(1, -inf, 0): i=1: skip → sum=0; include 1 → sum=1. From helper(1, 3, 1): i=1: skip → sum=3; include 1 invalid (1<3) so not called. Sums: 0,1,3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking to Explore All Subsequences
ladders.desc: Using recursion with include/skip decisions to generate all possible subsequences.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the backtracking technique to generate all subsequences of an array. The intuition is that each element in the array can either be included or excluded in a subsequence, leading to 2^n total possibilities. We use recursion to explore these choices: at each index i, we make two recursive calls—one where we skip the element (move to i+1 without changing the subsequence), and one where we include the element (add it to the subsequence and move to i+1). This creates a binary decision tree where each leaf represents a unique subsequence. The algorithm starts with an empty subsequence and recursively builds it. Time complexity is O(2^n) since we visit each subset, and space is O(n) for the recursion stack. This feeds into L1 by providing the framework; L1 adds the increasing constraint. Common mistakes: not handling base case correctly (when i reaches array length), or modifying global state incorrectly. Real code pattern: def generate(i, subsequence): if i==n: process subsequence; else: generate(i+1, subsequence); generate(i+1, subsequence + [arr[i]]).

examples.trace: Start i=0, subseq=[]. i=0: skip → call generate(1, []); include A → call generate(1, [A]). From generate(1, []): i=1: skip → base, subseq=[]; include B → base, subseq=[B]. From generate(1, [A]): i=1: skip → base, subseq=[A]; include B → base, subseq=[A,B]. All subsequences generated.
Start i=0, subseq=[]. i=0: skip → generate(1, []) → base, subseq=[]; include X → generate(1, [X]) → base, subseq=[X].

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion with State Parameters
ladders.desc: Implementing recursion that passes state (like index and accumulated values) through parameters.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to use recursion with parameters to maintain state across calls, essential for generating subsequences. The intuition is that instead of using global variables, we pass the current state (e.g., current index in the array, current subsequence or sum) as parameters to the recursive function. This makes the function pure and easier to reason about. The algorithm: define a recursive function that takes an index i and some state (e.g., a list for subsequence). Base case: if i equals array length, process the state (e.g., print or sum). Recursive case: call the function with i+1 and unchanged state (skip current element), and call with i+1 and updated state (include current element). Time complexity depends on operations but typically O(2^n) for generating all subsets, space O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to track the subsequence. Common mistakes: not copying state when including to avoid mutation, or incorrect base condition. Real code pattern: def recurse(i, state): if i==len(arr): use state; else: recurse(i+1, state); new_state = state + [arr[i]]; recurse(i+1, new_state).

examples.input: Array [7,8], function recurse(i, seq) with i=0, seq=[]
examples.output: Calls: recurse(0,[]) → recurse(1,[]) and recurse(1,[7]) → etc.
examples.trace: recurse(0,[]): i=0<2, call recurse(1,[]) (skip 7), call recurse(1,[7]) (include 7). recurse(1,[]): i=1<2, call recurse(2,[]) (skip 8) → base seq=[], call recurse(2,[8]) (include 8) → base seq=[8]. recurse(1,[7]): i=1<2, call recurse(2,[7]) → base seq=[7], call recurse(2,[7,8]) → base seq=[7,8]. All states: [],[8],[7],[7,8].

examples.input: Array [5], function recurse(i, seq) with i=0, seq=[]
examples.output: Calls leading to seq=[] and seq=[5]
examples.trace: recurse(0,[]): i=0<1, call recurse(1,[]) → base seq=[], call recurse(1,[5]) → base seq=[5].

testCases.expected: Final seqs: [x],[x,x]? No, start seq=[x], so outputs vary

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal with Index
ladders.desc: Iterating through an array using an index variable, accessing elements sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing an array from start to end using an index variable. The intuition is that arrays are ordered collections, and we can access each element by its position (index). We initialize an index i to 0 (the first element) and increment it until it reaches the length of the array n. At each step, we perform an operation on the element arr[i], such as reading its value or including it in a computation. This is basic but crucial for recursion where the index controls progression. The algorithm: set i=0; while i < n: process arr[i]; i += 1. Time complexity is O(n) for linear traversal, space O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the mechanism to move through the array in recursion. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (using i <= n), not checking array bounds, or modifying the array while traversing. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): # use arr[i].

examples.trace: i=0: arr[0]=10, print 10; i=1: arr[1]=20, print 20; i=2: arr[2]=30, print 30; i=3: stop.
i=0: arr[0]=5, sum=5; i=1: stop.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understanding subsequences as sequences derived by deleting zero or more elements without changing order.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept defines a subsequence of an array or sequence. The intuition is that a subsequence is obtained by selecting some elements from the original sequence while maintaining their relative order, but not necessarily consecutiveness. You can delete any number of elements (including zero or all) to form a subsequence. For example, from [A,B,C], subsequences include [], [A], [B], [C], [A,B], [A,C], [B,C], [A,B,C]. Key properties: order is preserved (e.g., [A,C] is valid but [C,A] is not), elements are not necessarily contiguous, and each element can be either included or excluded. This is crucial for the problem because we need to find subsequences that are increasing. The total number of subsequences for an array of length n is 2^n, since each element has 2 choices (include or exclude). This feeds into L4 by setting the stage for why we traverse arrays to build subsequences. Common mistakes: confusing subsequence with substring (which requires contiguous elements) or with permutations (where order can change). Real code pattern: conceptually, think of a binary mask over the array where 1 means include, 0 means exclude.

examples.trace: Start with empty []. Include/exclude each element: For 1: include→[1], exclude→[]; for 2: from [1]→[1,2] or [1], from []→[2] or []; for 3: combine all, e.g., from [1,2]→[1,2,3] or [1,2], etc. List all 8.
Only one element: include it → [X], exclude it → [].

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n²)
approaches.summary: Use a DP array where each element stores the maximum sum of an increasing subsequence ending at that index. Iterate and update based on previous elements. Suitable for medium-sized arrays due to its simplicity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence with DP O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding the maximum sum of any strictly increasing subsequence in an array using DP.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to build up solutions to subproblems: for each element, we want to know the maximum sum of an increasing subsequence ending at that element. We create a DP array `dp` where `dp[i]` stores the maximum sum of an increasing subsequence ending at index `i`. Initially, each `dp[i]` is just the element itself (a subsequence of length 1). Then, for each element `arr[i]`, we look at all previous elements `arr[j]` (where `j < i`). If `arr[j] < arr[i]` (ensuring increasing order), we can extend the subsequence ending at `j` to include `i`. We update `dp[i]` to be the maximum of its current value and `dp[j] + arr[i]`. Finally, the answer is the maximum value in the `dp` array. This works because we consider all possible ways to build increasing subsequences ending at each position, ensuring we capture the optimal sum. The time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested loops, and space complexity is O(n) for the DP array. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key subroutine of updating DP values based on previous elements. Common mistakes include not initializing `dp[i]` with `arr[i]`, forgetting the strictly increasing condition (`<` instead of `<=`), or incorrectly tracking indices instead of sums. Real code pattern: Initialize `dp = arr.copy()`, then nested loops: for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + arr[i]), return max(dp).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Updating DP Values for Each Element
ladders.desc: For a given index i, compute dp[i] by checking all previous indices j and updating based on increasing condition.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the DP solution. For a fixed index i in the array, we need to determine the maximum sum of an increasing subsequence ending at i. The intuition is that any increasing subsequence ending at i must have its second-to-last element at some index j < i where arr[j] < arr[i]. Therefore, dp[i] should be the maximum over all such j of (dp[j] + arr[i]), or simply arr[i] if no such j exists (i.e., starting a new subsequence). The algorithm: Initialize dp[i] = arr[i]. Then, for each j from 0 to i-1, if arr[j] < arr[i], update dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + arr[i]). This ensures we consider all possible extensions. Time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(i) for a given i, but in the full problem, summing over all i gives O(n²). Space is O(1) extra per element. Connection to L0: This subroutine is executed for each i in the full solution to build the DP array. Common mistakes include using <= instead of < for strictly increasing, or forgetting to initialize dp[i] with arr[i]. Real code pattern: dp[i] = arr[i]; for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + arr[i]).

examples.trace: Given arr=[3,1,4,2], i=3, arr[i]=2. Initial dp[3]=2. Check j=0: arr[0]=3 not < 2, skip. j=1: arr[1]=1 < 2, dp[3]=max(2, dp[1]+2)=max(2, 1+2)=3. j=2: arr[2]=4 not < 2, skip. Final dp[3]=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State Definition and Transition
ladders.desc: Define dp[i] as max sum of increasing subsequence ending at i, and transition using previous dp values.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is the DP state definition: let dp[i] be the maximum sum of any strictly increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i. This definition is powerful because it breaks the problem into overlapping subproblems. The transition relation: dp[i] = arr[i] + max(dp[j]) for all j < i such that arr[j] < arr[i], or simply arr[i] if no such j exists. Intuitively, to form an increasing subsequence ending at i, we can append arr[i] to any increasing subsequence ending at a smaller element j, provided arr[j] < arr[i]. We take the maximum sum among all such possibilities. This works because we consider all possible predecessors for each position, ensuring optimal substructure. The algorithm involves initializing dp[i] = arr[i] for all i, then applying the transition in nested loops. Time for this logic in isolation is O(n²) if we compute for all i, space O(n). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented by the subroutine that updates each dp[i]. Common mistakes include defining dp[i] as max sum up to i (which might not end at i), or missing the initialization step. Real code pattern: dp = [arr[i] for i in range(n)]; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + arr[i]).

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Maximum in an Array with Condition
ladders.desc: Given an array and index i, find max value in dp[0..i-1] where corresponding arr values are less than arr[i].
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves scanning a portion of an array to find a maximum value subject to a condition. Specifically, for a fixed i, we need to compute max_val = max(dp[j] for j in range(i) if arr[j] < arr[i]). If no such j exists, max_val is considered 0 (or we handle separately). The intuition is that we are searching for the best predecessor for arr[i] based on both the sum (dp[j]) and the increasing condition. This is a linear scan with a filter, taking O(i) time. In the full DP solution, this is done for each i, leading to O(n²) total. Space is O(1) for the scan. Connection to L2: This operation is used in the DP transition to compute dp[i] = arr[i] + max_val. Common mistakes include not checking the condition correctly or missing the case when no j satisfies the condition. Real code pattern: max_val = 0; for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: max_val = max(max_val, dp[j]); then dp[i] = arr[i] + max_val.

examples.trace: Given arr=[3,1,4], dp=[3,1,7], i=2, arr[i]=4. Initialize max_val=0. j=0: arr[0]=3 < 4, max_val=max(0, dp[0])=max(0,3)=3. j=1: arr[1]=1 < 4, max_val=max(3, dp[1])=max(3,1)=3. Final max_val=3.
Given arr=[5,6,4], dp=[5,11,4], i=2, arr[i]=4. Initialize max_val=0. j=0: arr[0]=5 not < 4, skip. j=1: arr[1]=6 not < 4, skip. Final max_val=0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Pairs of Indices with Condition Check
ladders.desc: Loop through all pairs (j, i) with j < i and check if arr[j] < arr[i].
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves nested loops to examine all ordered pairs of indices in an array, specifically those where the first index is less than the second. For each pair (j, i), we perform a condition check: is arr[j] < arr[i]? This is essential for the increasing subsequence problem because we need to compare each element with all previous elements to see if they can form an increasing sequence. The intuition is that we are exploring potential connections between elements where a smaller element can precede a larger one. The algorithm: for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: then perform some action (e.g., update dp). Time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested loops, space is O(1). Connection to L3: This iteration is used to find the maximum dp[j] under the condition. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop ranges or incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, n): for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: ...

examples.output: Pairs and checks: (0,1): 1<2 true; (0,2): 1<3 true; (1,2): 2<3 true
Pairs and checks: (0,1): 3<1 false; (0,2): 3<2 false; (1,2): 1<2 true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Increasing Subsequences
ladders.desc: Identify what an increasing subsequence is and compute sums of simple sequences.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding what an increasing subsequence is: a sequence of elements from an array where each element is greater than the previous one, and the elements appear in the same order as in the original array. For example, in [1, 3, 2], possible increasing subsequences include [1], [3], [2], [1,3], [1,2], but not [3,2] because 3 > 2. The sum of a subsequence is simply the sum of its elements. The intuition is that we are looking for such sequences that maximize the sum. This is a basic combinatorial idea: from an array, we can pick elements in order, skipping some, as long as each picked element is larger than the last picked one. The algorithm for this level is just to list all possible increasing subsequences and compute their sums, which is exponential in general but manageable for small arrays. Time and space depend on the number of subsequences. Connection to L4: This concept underlies why we check pairs with arr[j] < arr[i]—to ensure increasing order. Common mistakes include confusing subsequences with subarrays (which must be contiguous) or not requiring strict increase. Real code pattern: For educational purposes, use recursion or iteration to generate subsequences and check increasing condition.

examples.trace: Array [1,2]. All subsequences: [] (sum 0, but we consider non-empty), [1] (sum 1), [2] (sum 2), [1,2] (increasing since 1<2, sum 3). Max sum is 3.
Array [2,1]. All subsequences: [2] (sum 2), [1] (sum 1), [2,1] (not increasing since 2>1, so invalid). Max sum is 2.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain an array of the smallest tail values for increasing subsequences of various lengths, using binary search to update efficiently. Optimizes time by reducing comparisons in the DP process.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence with DP and Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete O(n log n) solution using dynamic programming with binary search to find maximum sum increasing subsequence.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence (MSIS) problem optimally in O(n log n) time. The intuition combines two key insights: 1) We need to track increasing subsequences by their ending values, and 2) For sequences with the same length, we want the one with the smallest ending value to maximize future possibilities. The algorithm maintains two arrays: 'tails' stores the smallest possible ending value for increasing subsequences of various lengths, while 'sums' tracks the maximum sum achievable for subsequences ending with that value. For each element in the input array, we use binary search to find where this element would fit in the tails array. If it extends an existing subsequence (found position), we update both tails and sums if we get a better sum. If it creates a new longer subsequence (position at end), we append it. The maximum value in sums at the end gives our answer. Time complexity is O(n log n) because we process n elements, each requiring O(log n) binary search. Space is O(n) for the tails and sums arrays. This approach is more efficient than the O(n²) DP solution because it reduces the number of comparisons needed to find where each element fits in the sequence hierarchy. Common mistakes include forgetting to track sums separately from tails, incorrectly handling duplicate values (should replace, not skip), and not understanding why we track smallest ending values. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete algorithm using the binary search helper function.

examples.trace:
Input: [1, 101, 2, 3, 100, 4, 5]
Initialize: tails = [], sums = []
Process 1: tails=[1], sums=[1]
Process 101: binary search finds position 1 (end), append: tails=[1,101], sums=[1,102]
Process 2: binary search finds position 1 (replaces 101), tails=[1,2], sums=[1,3]
Process 3: binary search finds position 2 (end), append: tails=[1,2,3], sums=[1,3,6]
Process 100: binary search finds position 3 (end), append: tails=[1,2,3,100], sums=[1,3,6,106]
Process 4: binary search finds position 3 (replaces 100), tails=[1,2,3,4], sums=[1,3,6,10]
Process 5: binary search finds position 4 (end), append: tails=[1,2,3,4,5], sums=[1,3,6,10,15]
Maximum in sums = 106

Input: [3, 4, 5, 10]
Initialize: tails = [], sums = []
Process 3: tails=[3], sums=[3]
Process 4: binary search finds position 1 (end), append: tails=[3,4], sums=[3,7]
Process 5: binary search finds position 2 (end), append: tails=[3,4,5], sums=[3,7,12]
Process 10: binary search finds position 3 (end), append: tails=[3,4,5,10], sums=[3,7,12,22]
Maximum in sums = 22

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search in Increasing Sequence Array
ladders.desc: Find insertion position in sorted array using binary search for DP optimization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core optimization that makes the O(n log n) MSIS solution possible. The intuition is that we maintain a 'tails' array where tails[i] represents the smallest possible ending value for an increasing subsequence of length i+1. This array is always strictly increasing by construction. For each new element x, we need to find where x belongs in this sorted array: either it replaces an existing element (if x is smaller than or equal to some tails[i]) or extends the array (if x is larger than all current tails). We use binary search to find the leftmost position where x <= tails[pos]. If x is larger than all elements, we return len(tails) indicating it should be appended. The algorithm works by maintaining left and right pointers, comparing x to the middle element, and narrowing the search range. Time complexity is O(log n) where n is the current length of tails. Space is O(1). This connects to L0 as it's the helper function called for each element in the main algorithm. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in pointer updates, forgetting to handle the 'append at end' case, and not understanding why we search for leftmost position (to maintain the 'smallest ending value' property). The real pattern uses standard binary search with careful termination conditions.

examples.trace:
tails = [2,5,7,9], x = 6
left=0, right=4
mid=2, tails[2]=7, 6<=7 → right=2
mid=1, tails[1]=5, 6>5 → left=2
left=2, right=2 → exit
Return 2 (position where 6 should replace 7)

tails = [1,3,5], x = 7
left=0, right=3
mid=1, tails[1]=3, 7>3 → left=2
mid=2, tails[2]=5, 7>5 → left=3
left=3, right=3 → exit
Return 3 (length of array, append at end)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Smallest Ending Values for Subsequences
ladders.desc: Track optimal ending values for increasing subsequences of different lengths.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight that enables efficient MSIS solving. The intuition: For increasing subsequences of the same length, the one with the smallest ending value is most valuable because it leaves room for more elements to be added later. Consider we have two subsequences of length 3: one ending with 10 (sum=20) and one ending with 5 (sum=15). Even though the first has higher sum, the second is better because any number >5 can extend it, while only numbers >10 can extend the first. So we maintain an array 'tails' where tails[i] = smallest ending value among all increasing subsequences of length i+1 seen so far. This array is always strictly increasing (tails[i] < tails[i+1]) because if we had a longer subsequence with smaller ending value, we could truncate it to get a shorter one with even smaller ending value. When processing a new element x, we find where it fits in tails using binary search. If x replaces tails[pos], it means we found a subsequence of length pos+1 ending with a smaller value. If x extends tails (pos == len(tails)), we found a longer subsequence. Time to process one element is O(log n) for search plus O(1) update. Space is O(n) for tails. This connects to L1 as it explains why we need binary search on tails. Common mistakes include not understanding why tails must be increasing, or confusing this with tracking maximum sums (which needs separate array).

examples.trace:
Start: tails=[]
Add 3: tails=[3] (length 1 ends with 3)
Add 1: 1<3, so replace: tails=[1] (better ending for length 1)
Add 4: 4>1, extend: tails=[1,4] (length 2 ends with 4)
Add 2: binary search finds position 1 (2<=4), replace: tails=[1,2] (better ending for length 2)

Start: tails=[]
Add 5: tails=[5]
Add 6: extend → tails=[5,6]
Add 2: replace pos0 → tails=[2,6]
Add 7: extend → tails=[2,6,7]
Add 3: replace pos1 (3<=6) → tails=[2,3,7]

ladders.title: Building Block — Tracking Maximum Sums for Subsequences
ladders.desc: Maintain parallel array storing maximum sums for subsequences ending with specific values.
ladders.explanation: While the 'tails' array tracks optimal ending values, we need a separate 'sums' array to track maximum sums. The intuition: When we update tails[pos] with a new value x, we need to know what sum corresponds to the subsequence ending with x. We maintain sums[i] = maximum sum achievable for an increasing subsequence ending with tails[i]. For each element x, we compute its potential sum: if we're replacing tails[pos], sum = (sums[pos-1] if pos>0 else 0) + x. If we're extending (pos == len(tails)), sum = sums[-1] + x (or just x if empty). We only update sums[pos] if this new sum is greater than current sums[pos]. This works because the subsequence ending with x must extend some previous subsequence of length pos (if pos>0). The sums array parallels tails but doesn't need to be monotonic. Time per element is O(1) after binary search finds position. Space is O(n). This connects to L2 as it adds the sum-tracking component to the ending-value tracking. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize sums properly, not handling the base case (pos=0) correctly, or updating sums when shouldn't (only update if new sum > old sum).

examples.trace:
Start: tails=[], sums=[]
Add 3: pos=0 (append), sum=3 → tails=[3], sums=[3]
Add 1: pos=0 (replace), sum=1 → tails=[1], sums=[1] (1>3? No, but we replace because 1<3)
Add 4: pos=1 (extend), sum=sums[0]+4=1+4=5 → tails=[1,4], sums=[1,5]
Add 2: pos=1 (replace), sum=sums[0]+2=1+2=3 → 3<5 so don't update sums, but update tails: tails=[1,2], sums=[1,5]

Start: tails=[], sums=[]
Add 5: append → tails=[5], sums=[5]
Add 6: extend → sum=5+6=11 → tails=[5,6], sums=[5,11]
Add 2: pos=0 replace → sum=2 → 2<5 so don't update sums, but tails=[2,6], sums=[5,11]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Updating Array Elements
ladders.desc: Fundamental array operations: access, compare, and conditional update.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations needed to implement the MSIS algorithm. The intuition: At its core, the algorithm repeatedly performs three operations on arrays: 1) Access an element at index i (O(1)), 2) Compare two numbers to determine their order (<, >, =), and 3) Conditionally update an element if a condition is met. In our algorithm, we access tails[mid] during binary search, compare x with tails[mid] to decide search direction, and update tails[pos] and sums[pos] if certain conditions hold. The update rule for tails is always replace with x when pos < len(tails), because we want the smallest ending value. For sums, we only update if the new sum (previous sum + x) is greater than the current sums[pos]. These operations are O(1) time each. Space is just the arrays themselves. This connects to L3 as these are the operations performed in the sum-tracking logic. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operators (e.g., <= instead of <), forgetting array bounds checks, or not handling empty array case. Beginners should practice these basic operations before tackling the full algorithm.

examples.trace:
Given: tails=[2,5,8], sums=[4,9,15], pos=1, x=6, previous sum (sums[0])=4
Step 1: Compare x=6 with tails[1]=5 → 6>5, but pos indicates replacement
Step 2: Update tails[1]=6 (always)
Step 3: Compute new_sum = prev_sum + x = 4+6=10
Step 4: Compare new_sum=10 with current sums[1]=9 → 10>9
Step 5: Update sums[1]=10
Result: tails=[2,6,8], sums=[4,10,15]

Given: tails=[3], sums=[7], pos=0, x=2, previous sum=0 (no element before)
Step 1: Compare x=2 with tails[0]=3 → 2<3, replacement case
Step 2: Update tails[0]=2
Step 3: Compute new_sum = prev_sum + x = 0+2=2
Step 4: Compare new_sum=2 with current sums[0]=7 → 2<7
Step 5: Don't update sums[1] (keep 7)
Result: tails=[2], sums=[7]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Increasing Subsequences
ladders.desc: Basic concept: what is an increasing subsequence and how to identify elements in order.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the core concept needed for the MSIS problem. An increasing subsequence is a sequence of elements from an array where each element is greater than the previous one, and they appear in the same order as in the original array. For example, in [3,1,4,2], [3,4] is an increasing subsequence (3<4, and 3 comes before 4 in array), but [1,2] is not because 1 comes after 3 in array (order matters). The 'increasing' means strictly greater (not equal). To check if a sequence is an increasing subsequence: 1) All elements must come from original array, 2) They must appear in same order as original, 3) Each element must be > previous element. The sum of a subsequence is simply adding all its elements. The maximum sum increasing subsequence is the one with highest total sum among all increasing subsequences. This is a prerequisite because the entire problem builds on this definition. Time to verify a subsequence is O(k) where k is length. Space is O(1). This connects to L4 as it defines what we're trying to optimize. Common mistakes include confusing subsequence with substring (subsequence can skip elements), forgetting order requirement, or allowing equal values (should be strictly increasing).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check elements from original: 1 and 4 are in [3,1,4,2] ✓
Step 2: Check order: In original, 1 at index1, 4 at index2 → 1 comes before 4 ✓
Step 3: Check increasing: 1<4 ✓
Step 4: Compute sum: 1+4=5
Result: Valid increasing subsequence with sum 5

Step 1: Check elements: 5 and 3 are in [5,2,8,3] ✓
Step 2: Check order: In original, 5 at index0, 3 at index3 → 5 comes before 3 ✓
Step 3: Check increasing: 5<3? No ✗
Result: Not increasing, so not valid increasing subsequence
